top_issues.title: Dual-mass flywheel and clutch failure on diesel models
top_issues.description: The dual-mass flywheel (DMF) and clutch assembly on 2.0 TDCi diesel variants can fail prematurely, sometimes within the first few thousand miles. Failures have been reported at 1,800 miles, 10,000 miles, 16,000 miles and 20,000 miles, with the DMF losing its damping medium and the clutch slipping or juddering. Repair costs are substantial and dealers have sometimes refused warranty claims, citing driver error.
typical_symptoms: Juddering or graunching noise when changing gear
Clutch slip under acceleration
Brake warning light (due to clutch slave cylinder failure leaking into brake fluid)
Difficulty engaging gears, especially second gear

top_issues.title: EGR valve and DPF regeneration faults causing limp mode
top_issues.description: Diesel models, particularly the 2.0 TDCi, can suffer from recurring engine service warnings and limp mode (reduced power) linked to the exhaust gas recirculation (EGR) system or diesel particulate filter (DPF) regeneration. Some owners have experienced the fault repeatedly even after multiple dealer visits and repairs, with the problem returning within days or weeks of collection.
typical_symptoms: 'Engine service now' warning light on dashboard
Car entering limp mode with half power
Hesitation when accelerating from low speeds
Fault recurring shortly after dealer repair

top_issues.title: 1.5 EcoBoost petrol engine overheating and failure
top_issues.description: The 1.5-litre EcoBoost four-cylinder petrol engine can suffer from localised overheating in individual cylinders, leading to warped cylinder heads, corrosion and complete engine failure. Some owners have been told their engine is not covered under Ford's EGR replacement scheme and have faced bills of £7,500 for a replacement engine. Coolant loss without visible leaks has also been reported before catastrophic failure.
typical_symptoms: Excessive coolant consumption (200–300 ml every few months) with no visible leak
Engine overheating warning
Loss of power or rough running
Engine failure requiring replacement

top_issues.title: Automatic gearbox (Powershift) repeated failures
top_issues.description: The automatic transmission on some models can fail repeatedly, even after replacement. One owner had the gearbox replaced in 2021 at a cost of £4,500, only for the same fault to recur 16 months later, with a second replacement quoted at £4,887. Additionally, some Powershift models have experienced engine shut-down issues and transmission oil changes have been quoted at £400.
typical_symptoms: Transmission warning light
Loss of drive or complete transmission failure
Engine failing to shut down on the ignition button
Hesitation or judder during acceleration

top_issues.title: Track rod arm fracture and suspension component failure
top_issues.description: A report exists of a track rod arm snapping clean in two whilst driving at low speed (15 mph) with no impact or pothole involved. This is a critical safety issue affecting steering control. Additionally, suspension components can wear prematurely, and the Kuga's firm ride characteristic can mask underlying wear.
typical_symptoms: Sudden loss of steering control or pulling to one side
Clunking or knocking from the front suspension
Uneven tyre wear
Difficulty steering or heavy steering

overall_reliability_note: The Ford Kuga Mk II is a practical and well-handling family SUV, but diesel variants are prone to costly clutch and DMF failures, EGR/DPF faults and automatic gearbox problems; the 1.5 EcoBoost petrol engine has shown overheating issues; manual gearbox models are generally more reliable than automatics.
